Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic, which I know sounds bad for someone my age who should have things figured out. I'd typically block out chunks of time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week, aiming for around 30 hours total. I'm very visual, so I'd create color-coded schedules and mind maps to organize topics. But between work commitments and life responsibilities, my actual execution of these plans varied wildly from week to week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I'd have good weeks where I stuck to my schedule religiously, then other weeks where everything fell apart due to competing responsibilities. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram in intensive sessions that probably weren't reflected accurately in my usual patterns. Being 28 and juggling multiple responsibilities means flexibility isn't always a choice—it's survival.
Q3: Honestly, I usually logged in when I had a specific task to complete or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes it was anxiety-driven—that nagging feeling that I was missing something important or falling behind. Notifications about deadlines or new materials would prompt me too. I'd also log in after connecting with peers in my study group to check what they'd referenced in our discussions.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became routine, usually early in the semester when my motivation was fresh. I'd check in every morning with my coffee, just scanning for updates. But that habit didn't stick consistently—my high stress levels and anxiety meant some weeks I'd avoid the LMS entirely because seeing my incomplete tasks felt overwhelming. The peer support group actually helped me get back into a rhythm when I'd fallen off.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as a visual learner—I watched over four hours worth because seeing concepts explained visually just clicks for me. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful, not for the content itself but for feeling connected to other students going through the same struggles. The peer interactions, even though I only had eight, were genuinely meaningful because they made me feel less isolated in my challenges.
Q6: I definitely ignored some of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs, especially when they felt like walls of text without visual elements. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely got through them. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped because they felt like busywork when I was already stretched thin. When you're managing high anxiety and stress, you have to be ruthless about what actually helps versus what just adds to your to-do list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assessments or when it genuinely helped me understand something I'd been struggling with. Visual content like diagrams, videos, or interactive elements always felt more valuable to me. I also valued anything that came recommended by my peer support group—if someone else found it useful, I trusted that endorsement more than the course outline.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, to be honest. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, so they got prioritized even when I was overwhelmed. Optional ones often got pushed to a 'someday' list that rarely got addressed. That said, when I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I genuinely wanted to, so the learning felt deeper and less pressured.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline during my commute or when I wanted to avoid the distraction of being online. I created extensive handwritten visual notes—mind maps, diagrams, flowcharts—that represented hours of study time completely invisible to the LMS. I also used external YouTube videos and resources when the course materials didn't explain something in a way that clicked for me visually.
Q10: So many situations! I'd watch a lecture once online, then replay my downloaded version multiple times while taking notes—the LMS only saw one view. My study group met in person at coffee shops where we'd work through problems together for hours. I'd also spend significant time just staring at my visual notes, making connections in my head, which obviously generates zero digital footprint but was crucial to my learning.
Q11: Constantly. My approach was to access materials once to download them, then work with them offline repeatedly. I'd print diagrams and annotate them by hand. Those video lectures I mentioned—I probably watched some sections five or six times offline, but the system only registered my initial access. It was actually better for my anxiety to study offline where I couldn't see reminders of everything else I hadn't done.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, usually when my stress and anxiety peaked. Mid-semester was brutal—I had competing deadlines from work, felt behind in the course, and my attendance dropped significantly. I'd look at the LMS and feel paralyzed by everything I hadn't done. The peer support intervention actually came at a crucial moment and helped me climb back from one of those low points.
Q13: My high stress and anxiety absolutely affected my LMS usage. Some weeks I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing my incomplete tasks triggered more anxiety. Fatigue from managing everything in my life meant I'd sometimes log in, stare at the screen, and log out without accomplishing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, personal life, health—meant the LMS often got attention only when absolutely necessary.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about deadlines or other life stuff. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. My anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged' with the materials.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around starting something imperfectly was sometimes worse than the stress of the deadline approaching. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, which was often just avoidance in disguise. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this pattern, even though I understood the material well enough to score decently on exams.
Q16: Deadlines created intense bursts of activity for me. I'd have weeks of minimal engagement followed by frantic last-minute sessions. The LMS would show me logging in repeatedly close to due dates, which probably looked like poor planning but was really just how I functioned under my stress levels. Deadlines were both motivating and paralyzing—the pressure helped me focus but also triggered my anxiety.
Q17: Feedback was actually really important to me, though I sometimes delayed looking at it because I was scared of what it might say. Positive feedback would boost my engagement significantly—I'd feel capable and motivated to tackle more. Critical feedback was harder; I needed time to process it without spiraling. The peer support helped me contextualize feedback better and not take it as personally.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear deadlines helped me prioritize, but the pacing sometimes felt relentless when I was already struggling. The availability of video content suited my visual learning style beautifully. However, the structure didn't account for students like me who might need flexibility due to life circumstances—it assumed a consistent engagement level I couldn't always maintain.
Q19: The video content and visual resources made engagement easier—they matched how I learn best. The discussion forums were accessible and helped me feel connected. What made engagement harder was the volume of reading materials without visual aids, and the lack of flexibility around deadlines. Also, the platform itself sometimes felt cluttered, which added to my cognitive load when I was already anxious.
Q20: I think that pattern describes students who are trying but struggling—maybe they're anxious about starting assignments, or they're searching for clarity they can't find. They might be logging in repeatedly hoping to feel ready to begin, or checking materials obsessively without being able to translate that into completed work. It's not laziness; it's often overwhelm masquerading as activity.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My exam score was decent despite my low engagement metrics because I was learning effectively offline and through peer discussions. The LMS captured maybe half of my actual study time. Students who know how to learn efficiently might not need to interact with every online resource—they extract what they need and work independently.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when students are active, what they access, how often they log in. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't capture whether someone was genuinely learning or just clicking through. They miss offline study, peer collaboration, the emotional state during engagement, and the depth of understanding versus surface-level interaction.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, making connections, and caring about understanding—not just completing tasks. For me, engagement includes the hours I spent creating visual notes, the conversations with my peer support group, and the moments when concepts finally clicked. It's an internal state that external metrics can only approximate.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my journey—how I climbed back from near-withdrawal to earning a Merit through sheer determination and peer support. It wouldn't capture my anxiety before logging in, the relief when I finally understood something, or the hours of offline visual studying that actually cemented my learning. It also wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention genuinely changed my trajectory this semester.
